news_summary: Today's news highlights inspiring achievements of women in science and emphasizes the advancement of women's rights and democracy in the digital age.
news_summary_fr: L'actualité d'aujourd'hui met en avant les réalisations inspirantes des femmes dans le domaine scientifique et souligne les progrès accomplis en matière de droits des femmes et de démocratie à l'ère numérique.

articles.title: ‘No one quite like her’: meet the female colleagues who inspire these award-winning women in science
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 02 March 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00666-xTo mark International Women’s Day, Nature asked winners of its awards programmes to nominate a colleague who brings out the best in them.
articles.content:
Science was once the domain of researchers toiling in solitude. Today, however, research thrives through collaboration, and is perhaps at its best when scientists recognize their colleagues’ contributions.

To celebrate International Women’s Day, held each year on 8 March, Nature asked six previous winners of awards given in partnership with Nature to name a woman who has had a positive impact on their career and well-being.

This year, Nature has focused on winners of the Estée Lauder Companies’ annual Inspiring Women in Science award, the inaugural Sony Women in Technology award — given to women who are using technology to drive positive change for society and the planet — and the annual John Maddox Prize, which is awarded in partnership with the UK charity Sense about Science to individuals who stand up for sound science despite challenges or hostility.

The six winners describe how the individuals that they nominate have not only advanced cutting-edge science but also improved the lives of those around them. These women, who span locations around the globe and several scientific disciplines, attest to how kindness, generosity and perseverance remain crucial to best practice in science.

ANITA MAHADEVAN-JANSEN

Biomedical engineer Anita Mahadevan-Jansen inspires others in how she balances professional success and personal joys — nominated by Jiawen Li (inset).Credit: Vanderbilt University (Main image), The University of Adelaide (Inset)

Job: biomedical engineer at Vanderbilt University in Nashville, Tennessee.

Research focus: optical diagnostics, neurophotonics and image-guided surgical techniques.

Nominated by: Jiawen Li, a biomedical engineer at the University of Adelaide, Australia, and commendation recipient of the 2025 Sony Women in Technology award.

J.L.: I had the privilege of sharing an office with Anita during her sabbatical at the University of Adelaide in 2019, the same year I gave birth to my first child. I have been fortunate to be mentored by many outstanding women, but meeting Anita at this crucial stage of my career had a profound impact. Her guidance helped me to navigate the challenging early years of academic motherhood, and her example gave me the confidence to push forward.

Women in science

Anita taught me how to set priorities, build practical to-do lists, share childcare responsibilities with my partner and, perhaps most importantly, be selective about when to say ‘yes’. For example, as women in a male-dominated field, we are often invited to serve on committees to improve gender representation. Although these opportunities are valuable, Anita demonstrated to me the importance of being selective: saying yes only when I can genuinely add value and create impact. She also showed me that it is possible to care deeply for one’s children while continuing to enjoy and thrive in a scientific career. Interacting with her and her adult children and seeing their pride in her reinforced my belief that we can ‘have it all’ in a smart and realistic way.

Over the past seven years, I have continued to witness how Anita embraces opportunities: translating her inventions from the laboratory to clinical trials, serving as president of the international society in our field and developing a medical device.

What has always stood out to me is how she balances these roles: she focuses on doing her best, rather than striving to be the best. Through her example, she has taught me to do this while continuing to enjoy what I do, rather than comparing myself to others or being consumed by self-doubt.

ANNA ABALKINA

Anna Abalkina hunts down fraud in publishing — nominated by Elisabeth Bik (inset).Credit: Stefanie Loos (Main image), Michel N Co, San Jose, CA, USA (Inset)

Job: integrity researcher at the Free University of Berlin.

Research focus: Russian governance, plagiarism and scientific publication fraud.

Nominated by: Elisabeth Bik, a microbiologist and scientific-integrity consultant in San Francisco, California, and winner of the John Maddox Prize in 2021.

E.B.: I have met Anna several times online and in person, and what always stands out to me is how approachable and generous with her time she is, even while tackling some of the hardest problems in science publishing.

Anna Abalkina: Fraud buster

Anna is now one of the leading experts in uncovering ‘paper mills’ that churn out fake manuscripts, plagiarism among doctoral theses, predatory publishers and ‘hijacked journals’. Her investigations have helped to remove fraudulent journals and papers from major databases, and have led to many corrections and retractions. She also co-founded the Retraction Watch Hijacked Journal Checker, which is widely used to help scientists avoid fake journals that mimic legitimate ones.

This work has come at personal cost. Through her earlier work exposing plagiarism and dissertation fraud in Russia, and through her continued investigations, she has faced intimidation and was even placed on a watch list by Russian authorities. She now lives in Germany and might never be able to return to her own country. Despite this, she continues to speak openly and work to improve scientific standards worldwide.

On a lighter note, however: last summer, Anna organized a visit for fellow science sleuths to a supposed paper-mill headquarters, which, it turned out, didn’t exist. We were attending a meeting in a European city, and Anna found a paper-mill company with headquarters listed at an address in that city. On Google Maps it looked like a small, nondescript office building. It turned into a fun side excursion showing that these companies can run out of fake addresses to use.

MAHA YUSUF

Mechanical engineer Maha Yusuf is an ‘unapologetically visible’ leader — nominated by Maheera Ghani (inset).Credit: Sundus Zahra (Main image), Maheera Abdul Ghani (Inset)

Job: mechanical engineer at Princeton University in New Jersey.

Research focus: advanced materials characterization and battery technologies.

Nominated by: Maheera Ghani, a materials scientist at the University of Cambridge, UK, and winner of the 2025 Inspiring Women in Science award.

M.G.: I first encountered Maha during my PhD, when I was still shaping my academic identity. Our initial connection wasn’t face-to-face, but when she travelled to the United Kingdom to conduct experiments — temporarily leaving her family behind — it became more personal. I remember being struck not only by her scientific focus, but also by the determination it took to make that journey.

As our conversations deepened, we realized how much we had in common: navigating male-dominated environments, balancing cultural expectations with ambition and building credibility in global research institutions. What began as professional admiration evolved into a genuine friendship marked by guidance and encouragement, and we remain in regular contact.

What stands out the most about Maha is her commitment to science with purpose. She was the first Pakistani woman to receive a PhD in chemical engineering from Stanford University, California — a milestone that carries both academic and symbolic weight. Her scientific contributions to energy materials are rigorous and have had an impact, but equally important is how she uses her platform. By remaining unapologetically visible, having work published at the highest level, mentoring generously and supporting younger women entering science and engineering fields, she quietly but firmly expands what leadership in science can look like.

KEI MAY LAU

Kei May Lau shapes semiconductor innovations and students — nominated by Yating Wan (inset).Credit: HKUST School of Engineering (Main image), Martins Bruvelis (Inset)

Job: electrical engineer at The Hong Kong University of Science and Technology in China.

Research focus: semiconductor materials and devices, photonics devices and power semiconductor devices.

Nominated by: Yating Wan, an electrical and computer engineer at the King Abdullah University of Science and Technology in Thuwal, Saudi Arabia, and winner of the 2025 Sony Women in Technology award.

Y.W.: I joined Kei’s group as a PhD student, and she has shaped the way I think as a researcher more than anyone else. She is known internationally for her pioneering work on semiconductor devices on silicon. This work helps make faster, more energy-efficient computing systems by enabling computers to communicate using light instead of electrical wires. Silicon chips run our computers, but silicon is very bad at producing light. Kei pioneered ways to grow laser materials directly on silicon, instead of attaching them later, which is more complex and expensive.

Behind every great woman in science, there’s another great woman in science

What I’ve learnt from her, however, goes far beyond technical skills. She taught me what it looks like to work with long-term vision and discipline, even when the field moves quickly and the challenges of achieving reliable, scalable and low-cost devices are enormous.

Kei has high standards and a very direct mentoring style. She trained me to think from first principles, starting from fundamental physics and material properties rather than relying on established assumptions or existing device templates. Under her mentorship, I learnt to ask why a device should work before asking how to fabricate it. This approach trained me to build solutions that were physically grounded and robust, and when experiments did not behave as expected, she pushed me to handle setbacks with resilience and professionalism. Those lessons have stayed with me through every stage of my career.
articles.reasoning: The article highlights inspiring women in science who have made significant positive impacts on their fields, advanced scientific integrity, and supported gender equality and mentorship in male-dominated disciplines. It focuses on tangible achievements, mentorship, and overcoming challenges, promoting diversity and inclusion in science with broad societal benefits.
articles.personality_title: Award-winning women in science celebrate the mentors who inspire them
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Science has changed from a solitary activity to one that thrives on teamwork and support. To mark International Women’s Day on March 8, the journal Nature asked six women who won science awards to name a female colleague who has inspired and helped them.

**What happened** – These six women come from different countries and scientific fields. They shared stories about women who not only made important scientific discoveries but also helped others grow. For example, Anita Mahadevan-Jansen, a biomedical engineer, mentored a colleague through the challenges of being a new mother and a scientist. Anna Abalkina works to catch fraud in scientific publishing, even though it has brought her personal risks. Maha Yusuf, the first Pakistani woman to get a PhD in chemical engineering from Stanford, supports young women in science. Kei May Lau teaches her students to think deeply and face problems with patience.

**Impact** – These stories show that science is about more than experiments and papers. It is also about kindness, helping others, and standing up for truth. The women nominated have helped their colleagues balance work and life, fight dishonesty, and become confident leaders. Their work improves science and makes it a fairer place for everyone.

**What's next step** – By sharing these examples, Nature encourages more scientists to support each other and create a welcoming environment. The women honored will likely continue mentoring and leading, inspiring future generations to follow their example.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Successful women in science highlight how mentorship, integrity, and support help both individuals and the scientific community thrive.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des femmes scientifiques primées rendent hommage aux collègues qui les inspirent
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La science est passée d’une activité solitaire à un travail d’équipe. Pour la Journée internationale des droits des femmes, le 8 mars, la revue Nature a demandé à six femmes lauréates de prix scientifiques de nommer une collègue qui les a inspirées et aidées.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Ces six femmes viennent de différents pays et domaines scientifiques. Elles ont partagé des histoires de femmes qui ont non seulement fait des découvertes importantes, mais aussi aidé les autres à progresser. Par exemple, Anita Mahadevan-Jansen, ingénieure biomédicale, a guidé une collègue pour concilier maternité et carrière scientifique. Anna Abalkina lutte contre la fraude dans la publication scientifique, malgré les risques personnels. Maha Yusuf, première Pakistanaise à obtenir un doctorat en génie chimique à Stanford, soutient les jeunes femmes scientifiques. Kei May Lau apprend à ses étudiants à réfléchir profondément et à faire preuve de patience face aux difficultés.

**Impact** – Ces récits montrent que la science ne se limite pas aux expériences et aux publications. Elle repose aussi sur la bienveillance, le soutien et la défense de la vérité. Les femmes nommées ont aidé leurs collègues à équilibrer vie professionnelle et personnelle, à combattre la malhonnêteté et à devenir des leaders confiantes. Leur action améliore la science et la rend plus juste.

**Prochaine étape** – En partageant ces exemples, Nature encourage davantage de scientifiques à se soutenir mutuellement et à créer un environnement accueillant. Ces femmes continueront probablement à encadrer et à inspirer les futures générations.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Des femmes scientifiques à succès montrent que le mentorat, l’intégrité et le soutien permettent à chacun et à la communauté scientifique de progresser.
articles.personality_title_es: Mujeres científicas premiadas celebran a las colegas que las inspiran
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La ciencia ha pasado de ser un trabajo solitario a uno que prospera con la colaboración. Para el Día Internacional de la Mujer, el 8 de marzo, la revista Nature pidió a seis mujeres ganadoras de premios científicos que nombraran a una colega que las haya inspirado y apoyado.

**Qué pasó** – Estas seis mujeres provienen de distintos países y campos científicos. Compartieron historias de mujeres que no solo hicieron descubrimientos importantes, sino que también ayudaron a otros a crecer. Por ejemplo, Anita Mahadevan-Jansen, ingeniera biomédica, apoyó a una colega a combinar la maternidad con la carrera científica. Anna Abalkina trabaja para detectar fraudes en publicaciones científicas, a pesar de los riesgos personales. Maha Yusuf, la primera mujer paquistaní en obtener un doctorado en ingeniería química en Stanford, apoya a jóvenes mujeres en la ciencia. Kei May Lau enseña a sus estudiantes a pensar con profundidad y a enfrentar problemas con paciencia.

**Impacto** – Estas historias muestran que la ciencia no es solo sobre experimentos y artículos, sino también sobre la bondad, la ayuda mutua y defender la verdad. Las mujeres nominadas han ayudado a sus colegas a equilibrar trabajo y vida, combatir la deshonestidad y convertirse en líderes seguras. Su trabajo mejora la ciencia y la hace más justa.

**Próximo paso** – Al compartir estos ejemplos, Nature anima a más científicos a apoyarse y crear un ambiente acogedor. Es probable que estas mujeres continúen guiando e inspirando a futuras generaciones.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Mujeres exitosas en la ciencia muestran que la mentoría, la integridad y el apoyo ayudan a que tanto las personas como la comunidad científica crezcan.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of six interconnected glowing orbs, each containing abstract scientific instruments like optical fibers, circuit patterns, and microscope lenses, linked by delicate threads of light symbolizing collaboration and mentorship, set against a soft, natural-toned background that evokes a nurturing, inspiring atmosphere.

articles.title: Press release - International Women’s Day: women’s rights and democracy in the digital age
articles.summary: To mark International Women’s Day, national and European parliamentarians will meet on Thursday to discuss stereotypes, disinformation and online gender-based violence.Committee on Women’s Rights and Gender Equality Source : © European Union, 2026 - EP
articles.content:
When: Thursday 5 March 2026, 9:00 - 12:30 CET

Where: European Parliament in Brussels, ANTALL building, room 6Q2 and via streaming.

On the occasion of International Women’s Day 2026, the European Parliament’s Committee on Women’s Rights and Gender Equality (FEMM), chaired by Lina Galvez (S&D, ES), will host an interparliamentary meeting with national and European MPs on “Women's rights and democracy: combating stereotypes, disinformation and violence in the digital age".

The event will be opened by Lina Gálvez, followed by a video message from European Parliament President Roberta Metsola. Opening remarks will be given by Hadja Lahbib, Commissioner for Equality, Preparedness and Crisis Management, and Florence Raes, Director of the UN Women Brussels Liaison Office.

The debate will feature contributions from journalists, policymakers and digital safety specialists, followed by a Q&A session with members of national parliaments and of the European Parliament.

The guest speakers are: Natalia Waloch, journalist and columnist at Gazeta Wyborcza and Wysokie Obcasy; Ana Natsvlishvili, member of the 10th convocation of the Parliament of Georgia and award-winning human rights expert; Dr. Silvia Semenzin, Adjunct Professor at the Open University of Catalonia; Julie Guichard, Digital Safety and Government Affairs Manager at Microsoft; and Carlien Scheele, Director of the European Institute for Gender Equality (EIGE).

The session will conclude with remarks by Josie Christodoulou, Commissioner for Gender Equality of the Cyprus Presidency.

See the full programme.

Seminar for journalists

On Thursday from 14.00, journalists are also invited to a seminar on digital violence against women. To attend the seminar in person or to get a link to ask questions remotely, please contact: media-seminars@europarl.europa.eu.

Here is the full programme of the event. You can also follow this event via streaming.

Background

A new EU gender equality strategy is scheduled to be adopted by the European Commission this week. The European Parliament adopted a resolution in November 2025 to provide its input to the strategy.
articles.reasoning: The article describes a significant and substantive event focused on advancing women's rights and democracy by addressing online gender-based violence, stereotypes, and disinformation, involving multiple European and national parliamentarians and experts. This initiative has broad societal impact and aligns with ongoing EU gender equality strategies, representing meaningful progress in human rights.
articles.personality_title: European parliamentarians unite to fight online violence and stereotypes against women
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Every year on International Women’s Day, people around the world focus on women’s rights. In 2026, the European Parliament’s Committee on Women’s Rights and Gender Equality planned a special meeting to discuss problems women face online, like harmful stereotypes, false information, and violence.

**What happened** – On March 5, 2026, national and European members of parliament gathered in Brussels and online. They listened to experts including journalists, human rights specialists, and digital safety managers from Microsoft. The meeting included speeches, a debate, and a question-and-answer session. Important figures such as Lina Gálvez, chair of the committee, and European Parliament President Roberta Metsola took part. Later that day, a seminar was held especially for journalists to learn more about digital violence against women.

**Impact** – This event brought together many important voices to talk clearly about the dangers women face online. It showed that governments and experts are working together to find solutions. The meeting supports a new European Union strategy on gender equality, which aims to make the internet safer and fairer for women. This cooperation is a key step toward protecting women’s rights in the digital world.

**What's next step** – The European Commission will soon adopt a new gender equality strategy. The ideas and discussions from this meeting will help shape that plan. Journalists and the public can follow these efforts online, and more talks like this are expected to continue pushing for change.

**One-sentence takeaway** – European leaders and experts gathered to fight online violence and stereotypes against women, helping to shape new plans for gender equality in the digital age.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les parlementaires européens unis contre la violence en ligne et les stéréotypes envers les femmes
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Chaque année, à l’occasion de la Journée internationale des femmes, l’attention est portée sur les droits des femmes. En 2026, le Comité du Parlement européen sur les droits des femmes et l’égalité des genres a organisé une réunion spéciale pour discuter des problèmes rencontrés par les femmes en ligne, comme les stéréotypes nuisibles, la désinformation et la violence.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Le 5 mars 2026, des membres des parlements nationaux et européen se sont réunis à Bruxelles et en ligne. Ils ont écouté des experts, dont des journalistes, des spécialistes des droits humains et des responsables de la sécurité numérique chez Microsoft. La réunion a compris des discours, un débat et une session de questions-réponses. Des personnalités importantes comme Lina Gálvez, présidente du comité, et Roberta Metsola, présidente du Parlement européen, ont participé. Plus tard dans la journée, un séminaire destiné aux journalistes a été organisé pour mieux comprendre la violence numérique contre les femmes.

**Impact** – Cet événement a réuni de nombreuses voix importantes pour parler clairement des dangers auxquels les femmes sont confrontées en ligne. Il montre que les gouvernements et les experts travaillent ensemble pour trouver des solutions. Cette réunion soutient une nouvelle stratégie de l’Union européenne sur l’égalité des genres, qui vise à rendre Internet plus sûr et plus juste pour les femmes. Cette coopération est une étape clé pour protéger les droits des femmes dans le monde numérique.

**Prochaine étape** – La Commission européenne adoptera bientôt une nouvelle stratégie pour l’égalité des genres. Les idées et discussions issues de cette réunion aideront à façonner ce plan. Les journalistes et le public peuvent suivre ces efforts en ligne, et d’autres discussions similaires sont attendues pour continuer à faire avancer le changement.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Des dirigeants et experts européens se sont réunis pour combattre la violence et les stéréotypes en ligne envers les femmes, contribuant à élaborer de nouveaux plans pour l’égalité des genres à l’ère numérique.
articles.personality_title_es: Parlamentarios europeos se unen para combatir la violencia y estereotipos en línea contra las mujeres
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Cada año, en el Día Internacional de la Mujer, se presta atención a los derechos de las mujeres. En 2026, el Comité de Derechos de la Mujer e Igualdad de Género del Parlamento Europeo organizó una reunión especial para hablar sobre los problemas que enfrentan las mujeres en internet, como los estereotipos dañinos, la desinformación y la violencia.

**Qué pasó** – El 5 de marzo de 2026, miembros de parlamentos nacionales y europeo se reunieron en Bruselas y en línea. Escucharon a expertos, incluidos periodistas, especialistas en derechos humanos y responsables de seguridad digital de Microsoft. La reunión incluyó discursos, un debate y una sesión de preguntas y respuestas. Participaron figuras importantes como Lina Gálvez, presidenta del comité, y Roberta Metsola, presidenta del Parlamento Europeo. Más tarde ese día, se realizó un seminario para periodistas sobre la violencia digital contra las mujeres.

**Impacto** – Este evento reunió muchas voces importantes para hablar claramente sobre los peligros que enfrentan las mujeres en línea. Mostró que los gobiernos y expertos trabajan juntos para encontrar soluciones. La reunión apoya una nueva estrategia de la Unión Europea para la igualdad de género, que busca hacer internet más seguro y justo para las mujeres. Esta cooperación es un paso clave para proteger los derechos de las mujeres en el mundo digital.

**Próximo paso** – La Comisión Europea adoptará pronto una nueva estrategia de igualdad de género. Las ideas y discusiones de esta reunión ayudarán a dar forma a ese plan. Los periodistas y el público pueden seguir estos esfuerzos en línea, y se esperan más charlas para seguir impulsando el cambio.

**Resumen en una frase** – Líderes y expertos europeos se reunieron para combatir la violencia y estereotipos en línea contra las mujeres, ayudando a crear nuevos planes para la igualdad de género en la era digital.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a diverse group of glowing, interconnected digital threads forming a protective web around a stylized European Parliament building silhouette, with abstract female silhouettes and symbolic shields representing digital safety and empowerment, rendered in soft natural tones.
